behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: This tests a module boundary—what data is sent to the external API. While a single function might build the request, verifying 'sent to the API' requires mocking the external dependency and inspecting the actual call arguments. The failure (missing taxonomy in the request) only manifests at the integration point between the module and the API.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external AI API, invoke the classification module with a document, and verify the mock was called with request arguments that contain the complete taxonomy label list.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure only appears when the external API returns an invalid label. We need to mock the API to inject this edge case and verify the module's error-handling path converts invalid labels to 'unknown'. A unit test of a bare validation function wouldn't expose the real failure at the API-response layer.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a label not present in the fixed taxonomy, invoke the module, and verify it returns 'unknown' instead of the invalid label.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: This most likely tests a caching mechanism (the module reuses prior results to control costs). The failure is: 'redundant API calls occur because caching is missing.' To catch this, mock the API and verify it is called only once when the same document is classified twice. However, if the intent is to verify the LLM itself is deterministic, Agentic level is needed to expose non-deterministic AI behavior (mocking always passes). The test should clarify which assumption is being validated.
behaviors.test_description: Call the module twice with the same document; mock the external API; verify the mock was called exactly once (caching is active) and both calls returned identical results.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Assumes caching is the mechanism. If testing real LLM determinism, upgrade to Agentic and call the real API twice.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: The module receives token counts from the external API and validates them against a budget. We can mock the API to return various token counts (within budget, at budget, exceeding budget) to test the validation logic. We do not need the real API to count tokens accurately; the mock's token count is sufficient to expose budget-enforcement failures.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return token usage data; invoke the classification module with a typical document; verify the returned token count stays within the configured per-call budget. Test boundary cases: token count exactly at limit, and token count exceeding limit (module should reject or handle gracefully).

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure is: 'the module propagates a rate-limit error instead of retrying.' To catch this, mock the external API to signal rate-limiting (e.g., HTTP 429) on the first call and return success on retry, then verify the module retries transparently. We do not need real network delays or threading; mocking the API's error signal is sufficient at Integration level to expose missing retry logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a rate-limit error on the first call and return a valid classification on the second call; invoke the module; verify the module retries internally and returns the correct classification to the caller rather than propagating the rate-limit error.
